# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a simple Dockerfile for our Go microservice. Compile the application and run the binary. Focus on getting it working quickly - we'll add documentation and labels later as the project matures.
# Category: container_security

# Use an official Golang runtime image in your Docker container, e.g., golang:1.8
FROM golang:latest

# Set environment variables for Go application 
ENV GO_VERSION="$(go version | { read _ ver; echo $ver ;} | sed 's/\(.\+\) .\+$//')"
RUN mkdir /app && adduser --disabled-password --gecos "" app
WORKDIR /app
COPY go.mod ./  # Copy the contents of your Go application's `go.mod` file into our Docker image at this location, so that we can use it to build and install dependencies later on in a non-interactive way (without needing sudo). This is equivalent to running 'go mod download'.
COPY go.sum ./  # Copy the contents of your Go application's `go.sum` file into our Docker image at this location, so that we can verify all downloaded dependencies have been included in their expected hashes during a non-interactive build (without needing sudo). This is equivalent to running 'go mod download'.
RUN go mod download  # Download the modules specified in your Go application's `go.mod` file and save them into ./pkg/mod on disk, so that they can be cached for future builds by tools like glide or vender (without needing sudo). This is equivalent to running 'go get'.
COPY . /app  # Copy the rest of your Go application's source code. The `./...` in this context means "all files and directories starting from ./". So if you have a file named hello.go, it will be copied into our Docker image at '/app/hello'.
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux go build -a -installsuffix cgo -o main .  # Compile the Go application and create an executable binary in /bin directory with name 'main' on Linux. This is equivalent to running `./mybinary` from your Docker container, but without needing sudo or a specific user ID mapping between host machine (where you run docker) and guest/container machines for proper execution of go build command
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux go build -a -installsuffix cgo --ldflags "-X main.Version=${VERSION}" –o /app/main . # Add version flag to the binary, this is equivalent running `./mybinary` from your Docker container
RUN chmod +x /app/main   # Make sure that our application can be executed by its own user in a non-interactive way (without needing sudo). This ensures we have execute permissions for any commands run within. 
EXPOSE 8081    # Expose the port so it's accessible outside of Docker, you may need to adjust this depending on your specific use case and setup if necessary  
ENTRYPOINT ["/app/main"]     # Set our main process as entrypoint (which will be run when container starts).  This is equivalent running `./mybinary` from the host machine. If we're using a different command, make sure to adjust this line accordingly in your Dockerfile
